title: What Should the Rest of the World Do?
text:
Such a future would be uncomfortable for America's allies, which are nowhere close to rivalling the likes of Anthropic. They are already vulnerable to Mr Trump's bargaining on trade, alliances, the dollar system and more. Al could become the most important lever of the lot. True, some of the depen-dence is two-way: America needs Dutch lithography machines and Taiwanese fabs. And foreigners could al-ways shun Anthropic or OpenAt in favour of good-enough open-weight alternatives.

Yet running models requires computing power, which America has perhaps 15 times more of than Europe, plus much more ongo-ing investment. If SpaceX, whose share price has surged after listing on June 12th, realises its vision of data centres in space, the gap is unlikely to close. "Europe 2031", a gloomy essay about the fu-ture of Al, imagines vassal status for Europe as its cyber-securi-ty, defence and swathes of its economy come to depend on American models and compute (see By Invitation).

Many countries will conclude they need America more than ever. They can still strengthen their bargaining positions. Compute is so lacking that it makes sense to build data centres almost anywhere, yet countries show no sense of what is at stake. In the seven months to June 2025 demand for new grid connections in Britain rose from 41GW to 125GW, more than twice peak power demand. OpenAl paused a data-centre pro-ject there in April, citing regulation and costly energy.

Token gestures

Throughout Europe more energy, easier planning and looser rules for modelmakers would help. In East Asia Taiwan, Japan and South Korea must integrate rather than duplicate their ef-forts. All should avoid the false promise of government at-tempts at steering investment. The goal should not be protec-tionism but ensuring that America is not the only economy where the At ecosystem can thrive. As in trade and defence, the way to cope with Uncle Sam's transactional turn is not toeйд whine about alliances but to build strength.

quizlet: достаточно хорошие модели с открытыми весами

questions.question: Why does the author believe America's allies may become increasingly dependent on the United States?
options: Because America controls most of the world's oil
Because America dominates advanced AI models and computing power
Because America produces all semiconductors
Because Europe has stopped investing in technology
questions.explanation: The article argues that America's lead in frontier AI and compute could become its greatest source of geopolitical leverage.

questions.question: According to the article, why is Europe's AI gap unlikely to close soon?
options: Europe has banned AI research
America has much more compute and continues investing heavily
European universities no longer teach AI
OpenAI has left Europe permanently
questions.explanation: The author points to America's huge advantage in computing power and continued investment.

questions.question: What problem does the article identify in Britain?
options: A shortage of AI engineers
Insufficient electricity infrastructure for new data centres
A ban on cloud computing
A lack of internet access
questions.explanation: Demand for grid connections has surged, while regulation and expensive energy delayed AI infrastructure projects.

questions.question: What does the author recommend for Europe and East Asia?
options: Restrict all foreign AI companies
Nationalise AI laboratories
Increase energy capacity, cooperate and strengthen their own AI ecosystems
Depend more heavily on American companies
questions.explanation: The proposed solution is greater investment, regional cooperation and stronger domestic AI ecosystems rather than protectionism.

questions.question: What is the main message of the article's conclusion?
options: Countries should wait for America to solve AI challenges
Governments should introduce stricter AI bans
Complaining about America is less useful than becoming technologically stronger
Open-weight AI should be prohibited worldwide
questions.explanation: The author concludes that nations should build their own technological strength instead of relying on or complaining about the United States.
